milt — Modern Integrated Library for Timeseries

Complete Interactive Documentation

Author

Gideon Nti Boateng

Published

March 24, 2026

1 Introduction

milt (Modern Integrated Library for Timeseries) is a unified R framework for time series analysis. Think Python’s Darts or sktime, but natively in R with a tidyverse-friendly API.

1.1 What Can milt Do?

Capability Functions
Create & manipulate series milt_series(), milt_split(), milt_window(), …
Forecast milt_model() → milt_fit() → milt_forecast()
Anomaly detection milt_detector() → milt_detect()
Change-point analysis milt_changepoints()
Classification milt_classifier() → milt_classify_fit() → milt_classify_predict()
Clustering milt_cluster()
Feature engineering milt_pipeline() + step DSL
Explainability milt_explain()
Causal impact milt_causal_impact()
Deployment milt_serve(), milt_dashboard(), milt_report(), milt_save()

1.2 Core Design Principles

Unified pipe-first API. Every function takes its main object as the first argument so you can chain with |>:

milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(series) |> milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

S3 outside, R6 inside. You never call $methods() directly. Use the exported verbs. The R6 objects live inside and manage state.

Lazy dependencies. Heavy packages (torch, xgboost, prophet, …) are only loaded when you use the backend that needs them — you get a friendly error message if the package is missing.

2 The MiltSeries — Your Data Container

Everything in milt starts with a MiltSeries object. It wraps your data with metadata: a time column, a value column, an optional grouping column, and the series frequency.

2.1 Creating a Series

2.1.1 From a ts object (most common)

# AirPassengers is a built-in R dataset — monthly airline passengers 1949-1960
ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers)
print(ap)
# A MiltSeries: 144 x 1 [monthly]
# Time range : 1949 Jan — 1960 Dec
# Components : value
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 2
  time       value
  <date>     <dbl>
1 1949-01-01   112
2 1949-02-01   118
3 1949-03-01   132
4 1949-04-01   129
5 1949-05-01   121
6 1949-06-01   135
# … with 138 more rows

You can also pass the frequency and start explicitly:

ap2 <- milt_series(AirPassengers, frequency = 12, start = c(1949, 1))
print(ap2)
# A MiltSeries: 144 x 1 [monthly]
# Time range : 1949 Jan — 1960 Dec
# Components : value
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 2
  time       value
  <date>     <dbl>
1 1949-01-01   112
2 1949-02-01   118
3 1949-03-01   132
4 1949-04-01   129
5 1949-05-01   121
6 1949-06-01   135
# … with 138 more rows

2.1.2 From a numeric vector

x <- milt_series(
  rnorm(60, mean = 100, sd = 10),
  frequency = 12,
  start     = c(2018, 1)
)
print(x)
# A MiltSeries: 60 x 1 [monthly]
# Time range : 2018 Jan — 2022 Dec
# Components : value
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 2
  time       value
  <date>     <dbl>
1 2018-01-01 107. 
2 2018-02-01  97.9
3 2018-03-01  94.4
4 2018-04-01 109. 
5 2018-05-01 117. 
6 2018-06-01 100. 
# … with 54 more rows

2.1.3 From a data frame / tibble

df <- data.frame(
  date  = seq(as.Date("2020-01-01"), by = "month", length.out = 36),
  sales = cumsum(rnorm(36, 5, 2)) + 100
)

s_df <- milt_series(df, time_col = "date", value_col = "sales", frequency = 12)
print(s_df)
# A MiltSeries: 36 x 1 [12]
# Time range : 2020-01-01 — 2022-12-01
# Components : sales
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 2
  date       sales
  <date>     <dbl>
1 2020-01-01  108.
2 2020-02-01  113.
3 2020-03-01  117.
4 2020-04-01  119.
5 2020-05-01  123.
6 2020-06-01  126.
# … with 30 more rows

2.2 Inspecting a Series

ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers)

# Basic metadata
ap$n_timesteps()    # number of observations
[1] 144
ap$freq()           # "monthly"
[1] "monthly"
ap$start_time()     # first timestamp
[1] "1949-01-01"
ap$end_time()       # last timestamp
[1] "1960-12-01"
ap$values()         # raw numeric vector
  [1] 112 118 132 129 121 135 148 148 136 119 104 118 115 126 141 135 125 149
 [19] 170 170 158 133 114 140 145 150 178 163 172 178 199 199 184 162 146 166
 [37] 171 180 193 181 183 218 230 242 209 191 172 194 196 196 236 235 229 243
 [55] 264 272 237 211 180 201 204 188 235 227 234 264 302 293 259 229 203 229
 [73] 242 233 267 269 270 315 364 347 312 274 237 278 284 277 317 313 318 374
 [91] 413 405 355 306 271 306 315 301 356 348 355 422 465 467 404 347 305 336
[109] 340 318 362 348 363 435 491 505 404 359 310 337 360 342 406 396 420 472
[127] 548 559 463 407 362 405 417 391 419 461 472 535 622 606 508 461 390 432
# Convert to a tibble for dplyr workflows
tibble::as_tibble(ap) |> head()
# A tibble: 6 × 2
  time       value
  <date>     <dbl>
1 1949-01-01   112
2 1949-02-01   118
3 1949-03-01   132
4 1949-04-01   129
5 1949-05-01   121
6 1949-06-01   135

2.3 Subsetting and Slicing

# Head / tail
milt_head(ap, 6)
# A MiltSeries: 6 x 1 [monthly]
# Time range : 1949 Jan — 1949 Jun
# Components : value
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 2
  time       value
  <date>     <dbl>
1 1949-01-01   112
2 1949-02-01   118
3 1949-03-01   132
4 1949-04-01   129
5 1949-05-01   121
6 1949-06-01   135
milt_tail(ap, 6)
# A MiltSeries: 6 x 1 [monthly]
# Time range : 1960 Jul — 1960 Dec
# Components : value
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 2
  time       value
  <date>     <dbl>
1 1960-07-01   622
2 1960-08-01   606
3 1960-09-01   508
4 1960-10-01   461
5 1960-11-01   390
6 1960-12-01   432
# Extract a date window
milt_window(ap,
  start = as.Date("1955-01-01"),
  end   = as.Date("1957-12-01")
)
# A MiltSeries: 36 x 1 [monthly]
# Time range : 1955 Jan — 1957 Dec
# Components : value
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 2
  time       value
  <date>     <dbl>
1 1955-01-01   242
2 1955-02-01   233
3 1955-03-01   267
4 1955-04-01   269
5 1955-05-01   270
6 1955-06-01   315
# … with 30 more rows
# [ operator — same as milt_window() but by position
ap[1:24]
# A MiltSeries: 24 x 1 [monthly]
# Time range : 1949 Jan — 1950 Dec
# Components : value
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 2
  time       value
  <date>     <dbl>
1 1949-01-01   112
2 1949-02-01   118
3 1949-03-01   132
4 1949-04-01   129
5 1949-05-01   121
6 1949-06-01   135
# … with 18 more rows

2.4 Train / Test Split

# 80% train, 20% test
spl <- milt_split(ap, ratio = 0.8)
cat("Train:", spl$train$n_timesteps(), "obs\n")
Train: 115 obs
cat("Test: ", spl$test$n_timesteps(),  "obs\n")
Test:  29 obs
# Split at a specific date
spl2 <- milt_split_at(ap, time = as.Date("1958-01-01"))
cat("Train ends:", format(spl2$train$end_time()), "\n")
Train ends: 1957-12-01 
cat("Test starts:", format(spl2$test$start_time()), "\n")
Test starts: 1958-01-01

2.5 Concatenation and Resampling

s1 <- ap[1:60]
s2 <- ap[61:144]
recombined <- milt_concat(s1, s2)
recombined$n_timesteps()
[1] 144
# Resample to quarterly (from monthly)
ap_q <- milt_resample(ap, period = "quarterly", agg_fn = sum)
print(ap_q)
# A MiltSeries: 48 x 1 [quarterly]
# Time range : 1949 Q1 — 1960 Q4
# Components : value
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 2
  time       value
  <date>     <dbl>
1 1949-01-01   362
2 1949-04-01   385
3 1949-07-01   432
4 1949-10-01   341
5 1950-01-01   382
6 1950-04-01   409
# … with 42 more rows

2.6 Handling Missing Values

# Introduce a gap then fill it
vals <- ap$values()
vals[c(10, 11, 12)] <- NA
s_na <- milt_series(vals, frequency = 12, start = c(1949, 1))

# Fill using linear interpolation
s_filled <- milt_fill_gaps(s_na, method = "linear")
print(s_filled)
# A MiltSeries: 144 x 1 [monthly]
# Time range : 1949 Jan — 1960 Dec
# Components : value
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 2
  time       value
  <date>     <dbl>
1 1949-01-01   112
2 1949-02-01   118
3 1949-03-01   132
4 1949-04-01   129
5 1949-05-01   121
6 1949-06-01   135
# … with 138 more rows

2.7 Visualising a Series

plot(ap)

3 Exploratory Data Analysis

milt_eda() gives you a quick statistical profile of your series — trend direction, seasonality, stationarity, and outlier counts.

ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers)
eda_result <- milt_eda(ap)
# MiltEDA
# Series  : 144 obs  1949-01-01 — 1960-12-01
# Freq    : monthly
## Descriptive Statistics
# A tibble: 11 × 2
   stat        value
   <chr>       <dbl>
 1 n         144    
 2 mean      280.   
 3 sd        120.   
 4 min       104    
 5 q25       180    
 6 median    266.   
 7 q75       360.   
 8 max       622    
 9 skewness    0.571
10 kurtosis    2.57 
11 n_missing   0    
## Stationarity
#  ADF  p-value : 0.01
#  KPSS p-value : 0.01
#  Likely stationary: FALSE## Seasonality
#  Detected period   : 12
#  Seasonal strength : 0.462
#  Has seasonality   : TRUE
print(eda_result)
# MiltEDA
# Series  : 144 obs  1949-01-01 — 1960-12-01
# Freq    : monthly
## Descriptive Statistics
# A tibble: 11 × 2
   stat        value
   <chr>       <dbl>
 1 n         144    
 2 mean      280.   
 3 sd        120.   
 4 min       104    
 5 q25       180    
 6 median    266.   
 7 q75       360.   
 8 max       622    
 9 skewness    0.571
10 kurtosis    2.57 
11 n_missing   0    
## Stationarity
#  ADF  p-value : 0.01
#  KPSS p-value : 0.01
#  Likely stationary: FALSE## Seasonality
#  Detected period   : 12
#  Seasonal strength : 0.462
#  Has seasonality   : TRUE

3.1 ACF and Decomposition Plots

milt_plot_acf(ap, lag.max = 36L)
milt_plot_decomp(ap)

3.2 Series Diagnostics with Recommendations

milt_diagnose() runs a suite of statistical tests and prints model recommendations.

milt_diagnose(ap)
# MiltDiagnosis
# Series    : 144 obs @ monthly# Range     : 1949-01-01 — 1960-12-01
Stationarity : stationary (CV ratio = 0.028)Seasonality  : seasonal (strength = 0.462, period = 12)Trend        : trend present (slope = 2.6572, p = 0)Gaps         : noneOutliers     : none
Recommendations:
• Significant linear trend detected. Models without detrending may underfit.• Seasonality detected (strength = 0.46, period = 12). Use a seasonal model (ETS, SARIMA, STL).

4 Forecasting

4.1 The Universal Forecasting Interface

milt uses the same three-step pipeline for every model:

milt_model("<name>")  →  milt_fit(<series>)  →  milt_forecast(horizon = <h>)

Let’s see what models are available:

list_milt_models()
# A tibble: 25 × 6
   name           description multivariate probabilistic covariates multi_series
   <chr>          <chr>       <lgl>        <lgl>         <lgl>      <lgl>       
 1 snaive         "Seasonal … FALSE        TRUE          FALSE      FALSE       
 2 ets            "Exponenti… FALSE        TRUE          FALSE      FALSE       
 3 nbeats         ""          FALSE        FALSE         FALSE      FALSE       
 4 auto_arima     "Automatic… FALSE        TRUE          TRUE       FALSE       
 5 knn            "K-Nearest… FALSE        TRUE          FALSE      FALSE       
 6 svm            "Support V… FALSE        TRUE          FALSE      FALSE       
 7 stl            "STL decom… FALSE        TRUE          FALSE      FALSE       
 8 elastic_net    ""          FALSE        FALSE         FALSE      FALSE       
 9 deepar         ""          FALSE        FALSE         FALSE      FALSE       
10 darts_transfo… ""          FALSE        FALSE         FALSE      FALSE       
# ℹ 15 more rows

4.2 Classical Statistical Models

4.2.1 ARIMA

Auto-ARIMA automatically selects the best (p, d, q) order.

ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers)

arima_fit <- milt_model("auto_arima") |> milt_fit(ap)
arima_fct <- milt_forecast(arima_fit, horizon = 24)

print(arima_fct)
# A MiltForecast <auto_arima>: horizon = 24# Forecast from: 1960-12-01# Intervals    : 80, 95%#
# A tibble: 6 × 7
  time       .model     .mean .lower_80 .upper_80 .lower_95 .upper_95
  <date>     <chr>      <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
1 1961-01-01 auto_arima  446.      431.      460.      423.      468.
2 1961-02-01 auto_arima  420.      403.      438.      394.      447.
3 1961-03-01 auto_arima  449.      430.      469.      419.      479.
4 1961-04-01 auto_arima  492.      471.      513.      460.      524.
5 1961-05-01 auto_arima  503.      482.      525.      470.      537.
6 1961-06-01 auto_arima  567.      544.      589.      532.      601.
# … with 18 more rows
plot(arima_fct)

4.2.2 ETS (Exponential Smoothing)

ets_fct <- milt_model("ets") |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 24)

plot(ets_fct)

4.2.3 STL Decomposition

stl_fct <- milt_model("stl") |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 24)

plot(stl_fct)

4.2.4 Theta Method

theta_fct <- milt_model("theta") |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

print(theta_fct)
# A MiltForecast <theta>: horizon = 12# Forecast from: 1960-12-01# Intervals    : 80, 95%#
# A tibble: 6 × 7
  time       .model .mean .lower_80 .upper_80 .lower_95 .upper_95
  <date>     <chr>  <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
1 1961-01-01 theta   440.      426.      454.      419.      461.
2 1961-02-01 theta   428.      410.      446.      401.      456.
3 1961-03-01 theta   490.      468.      511.      457.      522.
4 1961-04-01 theta   476.      451.      500.      439.      513.
5 1961-05-01 theta   480.      453.      507.      439.      521.
6 1961-06-01 theta   545.      516.      575.      501.      590.
# … with 6 more rows

4.2.5 TBATS

Good for complex seasonality (e.g. daily data with weekly + annual cycles).

tbats_fct <- milt_model("tbats") |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

print(tbats_fct)
# A MiltForecast <tbats>: horizon = 12# Forecast from: 1960-12-01# Intervals    : 80, 95%#
# A tibble: 6 × 7
  time       .model .mean .lower_80 .upper_80 .lower_95 .upper_95
  <date>     <chr>  <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
1 1961-01-01 tbats   451.      430.      473.      420.      485.
2 1961-02-01 tbats   440.      414.      467.      401.      482.
3 1961-03-01 tbats   509.      475.      547.      457.      567.
4 1961-04-01 tbats   496.      458.      537.      439.      560.
5 1961-05-01 tbats   498.      456.      544.      436.      570.
6 1961-06-01 tbats   567.      516.      624.      491.      656.
# … with 6 more rows

4.2.6 Prophet (Facebook)

prophet_fct <- milt_model("prophet") |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 24)

plot(prophet_fct)

4.2.7 Croston (Intermittent Demand)

Croston’s method is designed for intermittent (sparse) demand series.

# Simulate intermittent demand
demand <- c(0,0,3,0,0,0,2,0,5,0,0,1,0,0,0,4,0,0,2,0,
            0,0,1,0,3,0,0,0,2,0,0,1,0,0,5,0)
demand_series <- milt_series(demand, frequency = 12, start = c(2020, 1))

croston_fct <- milt_model("croston") |>
  milt_fit(demand_series) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

print(croston_fct)
# A MiltForecast <croston>: horizon = 12# Forecast from: 2022-12-01#
# A tibble: 6 × 3
  time       .model  .mean
  <date>     <chr>   <dbl>
1 2023-01-01 croston 0.882
2 2023-02-01 croston 0.882
3 2023-03-01 croston 0.882
4 2023-04-01 croston 0.882
5 2023-05-01 croston 0.882
6 2023-06-01 croston 0.882
# … with 6 more rows

4.2.8 Naive Baseline

Always a good baseline to beat.

naive_fct <- milt_model("naive") |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

print(naive_fct)
# A MiltForecast <naive>: horizon = 12# Forecast from: 1960-12-01# Intervals    : 80, 95%#
# A tibble: 6 × 7
  time       .model .mean .lower_80 .upper_80 .lower_95 .upper_95
  <date>     <chr>  <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
1 1961-01-01 naive    432      389.      475.      366.      498.
2 1961-02-01 naive    432      371.      493.      339.      525.
3 1961-03-01 naive    432      357.      507.      318.      546.
4 1961-04-01 naive    432      346.      518.      300.      564.
5 1961-05-01 naive    432      335.      529.      284.      580.
6 1961-06-01 naive    432      326.      538.      270.      594.
# … with 6 more rows

4.3 Machine Learning Models

ML models automatically build lag features from the series to train a regression model. You can tune how many lags to use.

4.3.1 XGBoost

xgb_fct <- milt_model("xgboost", lags = 1:24) |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

plot(xgb_fct)

4.3.2 LightGBM

lgb_fct <- milt_model("lightgbm", lags = 1:24) |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

print(lgb_fct)

4.3.3 Random Forest

rf_fct <- milt_model("random_forest", lags = 1:12) |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

plot(rf_fct)

4.3.4 Elastic Net

en_fct <- milt_model("elastic_net", lags = 1:12) |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

print(en_fct)

4.3.5 Support Vector Machine

svm_fct <- milt_model("svm", lags = 1:12) |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

print(svm_fct)

4.3.6 K-Nearest Neighbours

knn_fct <- milt_model("knn", lags = 1:12) |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

print(knn_fct)
# A MiltForecast <knn>: horizon = 12# Forecast from: 1960-12-01# Intervals    : 80, 95%#
# A tibble: 6 × 7
  time       .model .mean .lower_80 .upper_80 .lower_95 .upper_95
  <date>     <chr>  <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
1 1961-01-01 knn     401.      379.      424.      367.      436.
2 1961-02-01 knn     409.      387.      432.      375.      444.
3 1961-03-01 knn     414.      391.      436.      379.      448.
4 1961-04-01 knn     419.      396.      441.      384.      453.
5 1961-05-01 knn     437.      414.      459.      402.      471.
6 1961-06-01 knn     465.      442.      487.      430.      499.
# … with 6 more rows

4.4 Deep Learning Models (torch)

Deep learning models require the torch package with its Lantern C++ backend installed.

# One-time setup — downloads the C++ runtime
install.packages("torch")
torch::install_torch()

All DL backends follow the same pattern:

#| eval: false
# N-BEATS
nbeats_fct <- milt_model("nbeats", input_chunk_length = 24L, output_chunk_length = 12L) |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

# N-HiTS
nhits_fct <- milt_model("nhits", input_chunk_length = 24L, output_chunk_length = 12L) |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

# Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN)
tcn_fct <- milt_model("tcn", input_chunk_length = 24L, output_chunk_length = 12L) |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

# Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT)
tft_fct <- milt_model("tft", input_chunk_length = 24L, output_chunk_length = 12L) |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

# PatchTST
patch_fct <- milt_model("patch_tst", input_chunk_length = 24L, output_chunk_length = 12L) |>
  milt_fit(ap) |>
  milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

4.4.1 Checking the torch device

milt_torch_device()  # returns cuda if GPU available, else cpu

5 Working with Forecasts

A MiltForecast object stores the full forecast result — point estimates, prediction intervals, and (for probabilistic models) sample paths.

ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers)
fct <- milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(ap) |> milt_forecast(horizon = 24)

# Inspect
print(fct)
# A MiltForecast <ets>: horizon = 24# Forecast from: 1960-12-01# Intervals    : 80, 95%#
# A tibble: 6 × 7
  time       .model .mean .lower_80 .upper_80 .lower_95 .upper_95
  <date>     <chr>  <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
1 1961-01-01 ets     442.      420.      464.      408.      476.
2 1961-02-01 ets     434.      407.      461.      393.      475.
3 1961-03-01 ets     497.      461.      533.      442.      552.
4 1961-04-01 ets     483.      444.      523.      423.      544.
5 1961-05-01 ets     484.      440.      528.      417.      551.
6 1961-06-01 ets     551.      496.      606.      467.      635.
# … with 18 more rows
plot(fct)
# Extract as a tibble for further processing
tibble::as_tibble(fct) |> head()
# A tibble: 6 × 7
  time       .model .mean .lower_80 .upper_80 .lower_95 .upper_95
  <date>     <chr>  <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
1 1961-01-01 ets     442.      420.      464.      408.      476.
2 1961-02-01 ets     434.      407.      461.      393.      475.
3 1961-03-01 ets     497.      461.      533.      442.      552.
4 1961-04-01 ets     483.      444.      523.      423.      544.
5 1961-05-01 ets     484.      440.      528.      417.      551.
6 1961-06-01 ets     551.      496.      606.      467.      635.

5.1 In-sample Predictions and Residuals

m <- milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(ap)

fitted_vals <- milt_predict(m)
residuals   <- milt_residuals(m)

plot(residuals, type = "l", main = "Residuals", ylab = "Residual")
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)

6 Accuracy Metrics

milt ships a comprehensive suite of forecast accuracy functions.

ap   <- milt_series(AirPassengers)
spl  <- milt_split(ap, ratio = 0.8)
fct  <- milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(spl$train) |> milt_forecast(spl$test$n_timesteps())

actual <- spl$test$values()
pred   <- tibble::as_tibble(fct)$.mean
# Comprehensive accuracy report
milt_accuracy(actual, pred)
# A tibble: 5 × 2
  metric     value
  <chr>      <dbl>
1 MAE      30.0   
2 MSE    1282.    
3 RMSE     35.8   
4 MAPE      0.0653
5 R2        0.790 
# Individual metrics
cat("MAE:  ", milt_mae(actual, pred), "\n")
MAE:   29.97643 
cat("RMSE: ", milt_rmse(actual, pred), "\n")
RMSE:  35.80722 
cat("MAPE: ", milt_mape(actual, pred), "%\n")
MAPE:  0.06533672 %
cat("sMAPE:", milt_smape(actual, pred), "%\n")
sMAPE: 0.06682579 %
cat("MASE: ", milt_mase(actual, pred, spl$train$values()), "\n")
MASE:  1.423287 
cat("R²:   ", milt_r_squared(actual, pred), "\n")
R²:    0.789965

6.1 Probabilistic Metrics

# For probabilistic forecasts — need sample paths
# (Using simulated samples here for illustration)
samples <- matrix(rnorm(length(actual) * 200, mean = pred, sd = 15),
                  nrow = length(actual), ncol = 200)

cat("CRPS:    ", milt_crps(actual, samples), "\n")
CRPS:     22.94945 
cat("Coverage:", milt_coverage(actual, pred - 1.96*15, pred + 1.96*15), "\n")
Coverage: 0.5862069

7 Backtesting and Model Evaluation

7.1 Simple Backtest

ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers)

bt <- milt_backtest(
  milt_model("ets"),
  series         = ap,
  initial_window = 84,
  horizon        = 12,
  stride         = 12L
)

print(bt)
# A tibble: 3 × 5
  metric    mean      sd     min     max
  <chr>    <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>
1 MAE    23.4    11.7    13.8    43.5   
2 RMSE   28.1    13.3    16.8    50.8   
3 MAPE    0.0556  0.0233  0.0396  0.0967
tibble::as_tibble(bt) |> head()
# A tibble: 5 × 6
  .fold .train_n .test_n  .MAE .RMSE  .MAPE
  <int>    <int>   <int> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>
1     1       84      12  13.8  16.8 0.0396
2     2       96      12  19.7  24.4 0.0500
3     3      108      12  17.3  21.3 0.0450
4     4      120      12  43.5  50.8 0.0967
5     5      132      12  22.8  27.4 0.0466

7.2 Cross-Validation

cv_result <- milt_cv(
  milt_model("auto_arima"),
  series         = ap,
  initial_window = 72,
  horizon        = 12
)

print(cv_result)
# A tibble: 3 × 5
  metric    mean     sd     min     max
  <chr>    <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>
1 MAE    20.3    7.00   10.7    29.0   
2 RMSE   24.6    8.19   12.7    34.6   
3 MAPE    0.0543 0.0160  0.0329  0.0696

7.3 Comparing Multiple Models

cmp <- milt_compare(
  models = list(
    arima = milt_model("auto_arima"),
    ets   = milt_model("ets"),
    naive = milt_model("naive")
  ),
  series  = ap,
  horizon = 12
)

print(cmp)
# A tibble: 3 × 5
  model   MAE  RMSE   MAPE  rank
  <chr> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl> <int>
1 arima  18.3  21.2 0.0491     1
2 ets    22.2  26.4 0.0573     2
3 naive  63.8  78.6 0.163      3
plot(cmp)

8 Ensemble Models

Combine multiple models with averaging or weighted combinations.

ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers)

m1 <- milt_model("auto_arima") |> milt_fit(ap)
m2 <- milt_model("ets")        |> milt_fit(ap)
m3 <- milt_model("naive")      |> milt_fit(ap)

ens <- milt_ensemble(
  list(arima = m1, ets = m2, naive = m3),
  weights = c(0.5, 0.4, 0.1)
)

ens_fct <- milt_forecast(ens, horizon = 12)
plot(ens_fct)

9 Feature Engineering with Pipelines

The pipeline system lets you compose data transformations into a reusable, fittable object. Pipelines fit on training data and can be applied to new data — preventing data leakage.

9.1 Building a Pipeline

A pipeline always needs a model attached as its final step. Use milt_pipe_model() to attach one.

ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers)

pipe <- milt_pipeline() |>
  milt_pipe_step_lag(lags = 1:12) |>
  milt_pipe_step_rolling(windows = c(3, 6, 12), fns = c("mean", "sd")) |>
  milt_pipe_step_fourier(K = 3) |>
  milt_pipe_step_calendar() |>
  milt_pipe_step_scale() |>
  milt_pipe_model(milt_model("naive"))

print(pipe)
# A MiltPipeline [unfitted]# Steps : 51. lag2. rolling3. fourier4. calendar5. scale# Model : MiltNaive

9.2 Fitting and Transforming

spl         <- milt_split(ap, ratio = 0.8)
fitted_pipe <- milt_pipeline_fit(pipe, spl$train)

# Forecast using the fitted pipeline
pipe_fct <- milt_pipeline_forecast(fitted_pipe, horizon = 12)
print(pipe_fct)
# A MiltForecast <naive>: horizon = 12# Forecast from: 1958-07-01# Intervals    : 80, 95%#
# A tibble: 6 × 7
  time       .model .mean .lower_80 .upper_80 .lower_95 .upper_95
  <date>     <chr>  <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
1 1958-08-01 naive   2.73      2.27      3.18      2.03      3.43
2 1958-09-01 naive   2.73      2.08      3.37      1.74      3.72
3 1958-10-01 naive   2.73      1.94      3.52      1.52      3.94
4 1958-11-01 naive   2.73      1.82      3.64      1.33      4.12
5 1958-12-01 naive   2.73      1.71      3.75      1.17      4.29
6 1959-01-01 naive   2.73      1.61      3.85      1.02      4.44
# … with 6 more rows

9.3 Pipeline + Model

Attach a model as the final step of the pipeline:

full_pipe <- milt_pipeline() |>
  milt_pipe_step_lag(lags = 1:12) |>
  milt_pipe_step_rolling(windows = c(3, 6), fns = "mean") |>
  milt_pipe_step_scale() |>
  milt_pipe_model(milt_model("xgboost"))

pipe_fit <- milt_pipeline_fit(full_pipe, spl$train)
pipe_fct <- milt_pipeline_forecast(pipe_fit, horizon = 12)
plot(pipe_fct)

10 Individual Feature Steps

The milt_step_* functions work directly on a MiltSeries without a full pipeline.

ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers)

# Scale the series (z-score normalisation)
scaled <- milt_step_scale(ap)
print(scaled)
$series
# A MiltSeries: 144 x 1 [monthly]
# Time range : 1949 Jan — 1960 Dec
# Components : value
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 2
  time       value
  <date>     <dbl>
1 1949-01-01 -1.40
2 1949-02-01 -1.35
3 1949-03-01 -1.24
4 1949-04-01 -1.26
5 1949-05-01 -1.33
6 1949-06-01 -1.21
# … with 138 more rows
$step
# MiltScaleStep [zscore]
# Columns: value
# Add lag features (returns tibble)
lag_df <- milt_step_lag(ap, lags = c(1, 3, 12))
head(lag_df)
# A MiltSeries: 6 x 1 [monthly]
# Time range : 1950 Jan — 1950 Jun
# Components : value
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 5
  time       value .lag_1 .lag_3 .lag_12
  <date>     <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>
1 1950-01-01   115    118    119     112
2 1950-02-01   126    115    104     118
3 1950-03-01   141    126    118     132
4 1950-04-01   135    141    115     129
5 1950-05-01   125    135    126     121
6 1950-06-01   149    125    141     135
# Rolling statistics
roll_df <- milt_step_rolling(ap, windows = 6, fns = "mean")
head(roll_df)
# A MiltSeries: 6 x 1 [monthly]
# Time range : 1949 Jun — 1949 Nov
# Components : value
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 3
  time       value .rolling_mean_6
  <date>     <dbl>           <dbl>
1 1949-06-01   135            124.
2 1949-07-01   148            130.
3 1949-08-01   148            136.
4 1949-09-01   136            136.
5 1949-10-01   119            134.
6 1949-11-01   104            132.
# Fourier terms for seasonality
fourier_df <- milt_step_fourier(ap, K = 2)
head(fourier_df)
# A MiltSeries: 6 x 1 [monthly]
# Time range : 1949 Jan — 1949 Jun
# Components : value
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 6
  time       value .fourier_sin_1 .fourier_cos_1 .fourier_sin_2 .fourier_cos_2
  <date>     <dbl>          <dbl>          <dbl>          <dbl>          <dbl>
1 1949-01-01   112       5   e- 1       8.66e- 1       8.66e- 1          0.5  
2 1949-02-01   118       8.66e- 1       5   e- 1       8.66e- 1         -0.5  
3 1949-03-01   132       1   e+ 0       6.12e-17       1.22e-16         -1    
4 1949-04-01   129       8.66e- 1      -5   e- 1      -8.66e- 1         -0.500
5 1949-05-01   121       5   e- 1      -8.66e- 1      -8.66e- 1          0.5  
6 1949-06-01   135       1.22e-16      -1   e+ 0      -2.45e-16          1    
# Calendar features (month, quarter, weekday, …)
cal_df <- milt_step_calendar(ap)
head(cal_df)
# A MiltSeries: 6 x 1 [monthly]
# Time range : 1949 Jan — 1949 Jun
# Components : value
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 8
  time       value .year .month .quarter .week .day_of_week .is_weekend
  <date>     <dbl> <int>  <int>    <int> <int>        <int>       <int>
1 1949-01-01   112  1949      1        1    53            6           1
2 1949-02-01   118  1949      2        1     5            2           0
3 1949-03-01   132  1949      3        1     9            2           0
4 1949-04-01   129  1949      4        2    13            5           0
5 1949-05-01   121  1949      5        2    17            7           1
6 1949-06-01   135  1949      6        2    22            3           0

11 Anomaly Detection

11.1 The Detector Interface

Like models, detectors follow a two-step interface:

milt_detector("<method>")  →  milt_detect(<series>)

11.2 Statistical Methods

ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers)

# IQR-based detection (classical box-plot rule)
iqr_result <- milt_detector("iqr") |> milt_detect(ap)
print(iqr_result)
# MiltAnomalies [iqr]
# Series    : 144 observations  1949-01-01 — 1960-12-01
# Anomalies : 0 / 144 (0%)
plot(iqr_result)
# STL decomposition — flags residuals outside ±3 SD
stl_result <- milt_detector("stl") |> milt_detect(ap)
print(stl_result)
# MiltAnomalies [stl]
# Series    : 144 observations  1949-01-01 — 1960-12-01
# Anomalies : 6 / 144 (4.2%)# Anomalous times:
# A tibble: 6 × 3
  time       value .anomaly_score
  <date>     <dbl>          <dbl>
1 1958-07-01   491           3.17
2 1958-08-01   505           3.36
3 1959-07-01   548           3.54
4 1959-08-01   559           3.53
5 1960-07-01   622           4.39
6 1960-08-01   606           3.44
# Grubbs test (univariate extreme value)
grubbs_result <- milt_detector("grubbs") |> milt_detect(ap)
print(grubbs_result)
# Generalised ESD test (multiple outliers)
gesd_result <- milt_detector("gesd") |> milt_detect(ap)
print(gesd_result)
# MiltAnomalies [gesd]
# Series    : 144 observations  1949-01-01 — 1960-12-01
# Anomalies : 0 / 144 (0%)

11.3 Machine Learning Methods

# Local Outlier Factor
lof_result <- milt_detector("lof") |> milt_detect(ap)
print(lof_result)
# Isolation Forest
iforest_result <- milt_detector("isolation_forest") |> milt_detect(ap)
print(iforest_result)
# Autoencoder (deep learning)
ae_result <- milt_detector("autoencoder") |> milt_detect(ap)
print(ae_result)

11.4 Ensemble Detection

Combine multiple detectors — a point is anomalous only if a majority vote says so.

ens_result <- milt_detector("ensemble",
  detectors = list(
    milt_detector("iqr"),
    milt_detector("gesd")
  )
) |> milt_detect(ap)
print(ens_result)
# MiltAnomalies [ensemble]
# Series    : 144 observations  1949-01-01 — 1960-12-01
# Anomalies : 0 / 144 (0%)

11.5 MiltAnomalies Result Object

result <- milt_detector("iqr") |> milt_detect(ap)

# Convert to tibble
tibble::as_tibble(result) |> dplyr::filter(is_anomaly) |> head()
# A tibble: 0 × 6
# ℹ 6 variables: time <date>, value <dbl>, .is_anomaly <lgl>,
#   .anomaly_score <dbl>, is_anomaly <lgl>, anomaly_score <dbl>

12 Change-Point Detection

ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers)

cp_result <- milt_changepoints(ap, method = "pelt")
print(cp_result)
plot(cp_result)

13 Multi-Series Analysis

13.1 Creating a Multi-Series Object

# Simulate three store sales series
set.seed(42)
sales_df <- data.frame(
  date  = rep(seq(as.Date("2019-01-01"), by = "month", length.out = 48), 3),
  store = rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 48),
  sales = c(
    100 + cumsum(rnorm(48, 1, 3)),
    200 + cumsum(rnorm(48, 2, 5)),
    150 + cumsum(rnorm(48, 0.5, 2))
  )
)

ms <- milt_series(sales_df,
  time_col  = "date",
  value_col = "sales",
  group_col = "store",
  frequency = 12
)

print(ms)
# A MiltSeries: 48 x 1 [12] | 3 series
# Time range : 2019-01-01 — 2022-12-01
# Components : sales
# Groups     : store (3 series)
# Gaps       : none
# A tibble: 6 × 3
  date       store sales
  <date>     <chr> <dbl>
1 2019-01-01 A      105.
2 2019-02-01 A      104.
3 2019-03-01 A      107.
4 2019-04-01 A      109.
5 2019-05-01 A      112.
6 2019-06-01 A      112.
# … with 138 more rows

13.2 Fitting Models Across All Groups

# Fit ETS to every store independently
ms_fit <- milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(ms)
ms_fct <- milt_forecast(ms_fit, horizon = 12)
print(ms_fct)
# A MiltForecast <ets>: horizon = 12# Forecast from: 2022-12-01# Intervals    : 80, 95%#
# A tibble: 6 × 7
  time       .model .mean .lower_80 .upper_80 .lower_95 .upper_95
  <date>     <chr>  <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
1 2022-12-31 ets     156.      144.      169.      138.      175.
2 2023-01-30 ets     156.      139.      174.      130.      183.
3 2023-03-01 ets     156.      135.      178.      124.      189.
4 2023-03-31 ets     156.      132.      181.      119.      194.
5 2023-04-30 ets     156.      129.      184.      114.      198.
6 2023-05-30 ets     156.      126.      187.      110.      203.
# … with 6 more rows

14 Hierarchical Reconciliation

When your series form a hierarchy (e.g., country → region → store), forecasts at lower levels should sum to the forecast at the higher level. milt_reconcile() enforces this.

set.seed(42)
# Three individual series: total, region_A, region_B
make_hier <- function(n, mean_drift) {
  milt_series(300 + cumsum(rnorm(n, mean_drift, 3)),
              frequency = 12, start = c(2019, 1))
}

s_total    <- make_hier(48, 3)
s_region_a <- make_hier(48, 1)
s_region_b <- make_hier(48, 2)

# Fit ETS independently on each series
fct_total    <- milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(s_total)    |> milt_forecast(horizon = 12)
fct_region_a <- milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(s_region_a) |> milt_forecast(horizon = 12)
fct_region_b <- milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(s_region_b) |> milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

# Summation matrix S: rows = [total, region_A, region_B], cols = [region_A, region_B]
S <- matrix(
  c(1, 1,   # total  = region_A + region_B
    1, 0,   # region_A
    0, 1),  # region_B
  nrow = 3, ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE
)

# Reconcile — forecasts is a named list of MiltForecast objects
reconciled <- milt_reconcile(
  forecasts = list(total = fct_total, region_A = fct_region_a, region_B = fct_region_b),
  S         = S,
  method    = "ols"
)
print(reconciled)
# MiltReconciliation [ols]
# Series: total, region_A, region_B
tibble::as_tibble(reconciled)
# A tibble: 36 × 3
   series     h .mean
   <chr>  <int> <dbl>
 1 total      1  541.
 2 total      2  544.
 3 total      3  547.
 4 total      4  550.
 5 total      5  552.
 6 total      6  555.
 7 total      7  558.
 8 total      8  561.
 9 total      9  564.
10 total     10  566.
# ℹ 26 more rows

15 Time Series Classification

Classify time series into predefined categories. Useful for product type detection, fault classification, customer segmentation, and more.

# Build labelled training data
set.seed(1)
make_series <- function(n, trend, noise) {
  milt_series(cumsum(rnorm(n, trend, noise)),
    frequency = 1, start = c(2020, 1))
}

# Three classes: trending up, flat, trending down
train_series <- list(
  s1 = make_series(50,  0.5, 1),
  s2 = make_series(50,  0.0, 1),
  s3 = make_series(50, -0.5, 1),
  s4 = make_series(50,  0.6, 1),
  s5 = make_series(50, -0.4, 1)
)

labels <- c("up", "flat", "down", "up", "down")

# Fit classifier
clf <- milt_classifier("random_forest") |>
  milt_classify_fit(train_series, labels)

# Predict on new series
new_series <- list(
  t1 = make_series(50,  0.4, 1),
  t2 = make_series(50, -0.3, 1)
)

predictions <- milt_classify_predict(clf, new_series)
print(predictions)

16 Time Series Clustering

Group similar time series together without labels.

# Simulate 9 series from 3 underlying patterns
set.seed(42)
series_list <- lapply(1:9, function(i) {
  group   <- (i - 1) %% 3
  trend   <- c(0.3, 0, -0.3)[group + 1]
  vals    <- cumsum(rnorm(60, trend, 1))
  milt_series(vals, frequency = 12, start = c(2018, 1))
})

cl_result <- milt_cluster(series_list, k = 3, method = "dtw")
print(cl_result)

17 Explainability

milt_explain() uses SHAP-style feature importance to show which lag/feature values drove each forecast.

ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers)

xgb_model <- milt_model("xgboost", lags = 1:12) |> milt_fit(ap)
explanation <- milt_explain(xgb_model, ap)

print(explanation)
plot(explanation)

18 Causal Impact Analysis

Measure the effect of an intervention (e.g., a marketing campaign, product launch, policy change) by building a counterfactual forecast of what would have happened without it.

set.seed(1)
n        <- 100
pre_n    <- 70
baseline <- cumsum(rnorm(n, 0.5, 1)) + 100

# Add a treatment effect from observation 71 onwards
treatment                       <- baseline
treatment[(pre_n + 1):n] <- treatment[(pre_n + 1):n] + 15

dates  <- seq(as.Date("2016-01-01"), by = "week", length.out = n)
series <- milt_series(treatment, frequency = 52, start = c(2016, 1))

ci_result <- milt_causal_impact(
  series,
  event_time = dates[pre_n + 1]
)

print(ci_result)
plot(ci_result)

19 Saving and Loading Models

Trained models can be serialised to disk and reloaded in a fresh session.

ap    <- milt_series(AirPassengers)
model <- milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(ap)

# Save to a file
tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".milt")
milt_save(model, path = tmp)

# Load from file
loaded <- milt_load(tmp)
fct    <- milt_forecast(loaded, horizon = 12)
print(fct)
# A MiltForecast <ets>: horizon = 12# Forecast from: 1960-12-01# Intervals    : 80, 95%#
# A tibble: 6 × 7
  time       .model .mean .lower_80 .upper_80 .lower_95 .upper_95
  <date>     <chr>  <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
1 1961-01-01 ets     442.      420.      464.      408.      476.
2 1961-02-01 ets     434.      407.      461.      393.      475.
3 1961-03-01 ets     497.      461.      533.      442.      552.
4 1961-04-01 ets     483.      444.      523.      423.      544.
5 1961-05-01 ets     484.      440.      528.      417.      551.
6 1961-06-01 ets     551.      496.      606.      467.      635.
# … with 6 more rows
# Clean up
file.remove(tmp)
[1] TRUE

20 Model Registry

milt has a global model registry. You can inspect it and register your own custom model backends.

# Inspect all registered models
models_tbl <- list_milt_models()
print(models_tbl)
# A tibble: 25 × 6
   name           description multivariate probabilistic covariates multi_series
   <chr>          <chr>       <lgl>        <lgl>         <lgl>      <lgl>       
 1 snaive         "Seasonal … FALSE        TRUE          FALSE      FALSE       
 2 ets            "Exponenti… FALSE        TRUE          FALSE      FALSE       
 3 nbeats         ""          FALSE        FALSE         FALSE      FALSE       
 4 auto_arima     "Automatic… FALSE        TRUE          TRUE       FALSE       
 5 knn            "K-Nearest… FALSE        TRUE          FALSE      FALSE       
 6 svm            "Support V… FALSE        TRUE          FALSE      FALSE       
 7 stl            "STL decom… FALSE        TRUE          FALSE      FALSE       
 8 elastic_net    ""          FALSE        FALSE         FALSE      FALSE       
 9 deepar         ""          FALSE        FALSE         FALSE      FALSE       
10 darts_transfo… ""          FALSE        FALSE         FALSE      FALSE       
# ℹ 15 more rows

20.1 Registering a Custom Model

You can register any R6 class that follows the MiltModelBase interface:

# Minimal custom model skeleton
MyCustomModel <- R6::R6Class(
  classname = "MyCustomModel",
  inherit   = MiltModelBase,
  public = list(
    fit = function(series) {
      # ... fit logic ...
      private$.is_fitted <- TRUE
      invisible(self)
    },
    forecast = function(horizon, ...) {
      # ... forecast logic ...
    },
    predict = function(series = NULL, ...) {
      # ... in-sample prediction ...
    },
    residuals = function(...) {
      # ... residuals ...
    }
  )
)

# Register it
register_milt_model(
  name        = "my_model",
  class       = MyCustomModel,
  description = "My custom model"
)

# Use it just like any built-in model
milt_model("my_model") |> milt_fit(ap) |> milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

21 Deployment

21.1 Generating a Report

milt_report() renders an HTML / PDF report from a fitted forecast.

# Requires pandoc and rmarkdown
ap    <- milt_series(AirPassengers)
model <- milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(ap)

milt_report(
  series      = ap,
  models      = list(ets = model),
  horizon     = 24L,
  output_format = "html",
  output_file   = "my_forecast_report.html"
)

21.2 Interactive Dashboard

milt_dashboard() launches a Shiny dashboard to explore forecasts interactively.

# Requires shiny
milt_dashboard(
  model  = model,
  series = ap
)

21.3 REST API with Plumber

milt_serve() wraps a trained model as a REST API using the plumber package.

# Requires plumber
api <- milt_serve(model, port = 8080)

# Then call your model:
# POST http://localhost:8080/predict  {"horizon": 12}

22 Deep Dive: The Architecture

Understanding how milt is structured helps when extending it.

22.1 4-Layer Architecture

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Layer 1: User API                               │
│  milt_model(), milt_fit(), milt_forecast(), …    │
│  S3 generics: print(), plot(), summary()         │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 2: Core Engine                            │
│  MiltSeriesR6, MiltForecastR6, ModelRegistry     │
│  MiltAnomaliesR6, MiltBacktestR6, …              │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 3: Model Backends                         │
│  backend-arima.R, backend-ets.R, backend-xgb.R  │
│  backend-nbeats.R, detect-iqr.R, …              │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 4: Data Layer                             │
│  tsibble, arrow, data.table, tibble              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

22.2 Adding a New Model Backend

  1. Create R/backend-mymodel.R
  2. Define an R6 class inheriting MiltModelBase
  3. Implement fit(), forecast(), predict(), residuals()
  4. Call register_milt_model() at package load time
  5. Done — no changes needed anywhere else

22.3 The Dependency Strategy

Type Packages Behaviour
Imports R6, rlang, cli, dplyr, tibble, tsibble, … Always available
Suggests forecast, xgboost, torch, prophet, … Loaded on demand via check_installed_backend()

23 Complete End-to-End Example

Let’s walk through a complete forecasting workflow — from raw data to deployed forecast — in one coherent example.

# ── 1. Create the series ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers, frequency = 12, start = c(1949, 1))
print(ap)

# ── 2. Explore ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
eda_result <- milt_eda(ap)
print(eda_result)

# ── 3. Diagnose ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
milt_diagnose(ap)

# ── 4. Split ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
spl <- milt_split(ap, ratio = 0.8)

# ── 5. Detect anomalies on training data ─────────────────────────────────────
anomalies <- milt_detector("iqr") |> milt_detect(spl$train)
print(anomalies)

# ── 6. Fit and compare three models ──────────────────────────────────────────
cmp <- milt_compare(
  models = list(
    ets   = milt_model("ets"),
    arima = milt_model("auto_arima"),
    knn   = milt_model("knn", lags = 1:12)
  ),
  series  = spl$train,
  horizon = spl$test$n_timesteps()
)
print(cmp)

# ── 7. Pick best and forecast ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
best_model <- milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(ap)
final_fct  <- milt_forecast(best_model, horizon = 24)

plot(final_fct)

# ── 8. Score on held-out test set ─────────────────────────────────────────────
test_fct <- milt_model("ets") |>
  milt_fit(spl$train) |>
  milt_forecast(spl$test$n_timesteps())

milt_accuracy(spl$test$values(), tibble::as_tibble(test_fct)$.mean)

24 Quick Reference

24.1 Series Functions

Function Purpose
milt_series() Create a MiltSeries
milt_head() / milt_tail() First / last N observations
milt_window() Subset by date range
milt_split() Train/test split by ratio
milt_split_at() Train/test split by date
milt_concat() Join two series
milt_resample() Change frequency
milt_fill_gaps() Interpolate missing values
milt_to_tibble() Convert to tibble
milt_to_ts() Convert to base R ts
milt_to_tsibble() Convert to tsibble

24.2 Model Functions

Function Purpose
list_milt_models() Show all registered models
milt_model(name) Instantiate a model
milt_fit(model, series) Train the model
milt_forecast(model, horizon) Generate forecasts
milt_predict(model) In-sample fitted values
milt_residuals(model) Training residuals
milt_backtest() Rolling-window backtest
milt_cv() Time-series cross-validation
milt_compare() Side-by-side model comparison
milt_ensemble() Weighted model ensemble

24.3 Model Names

Name Type Package
"auto_arima" Classical forecast
"ets" Classical forecast
"stl" Classical forecast
"theta" Classical forecast
"tbats" Classical forecast
"prophet" Classical prophet
"croston" Classical forecast
"naive" Baseline —
"xgboost" ML xgboost
"lightgbm" ML lightgbm
"random_forest" ML ranger
"elastic_net" ML glmnet
"svm" ML e1071
"knn" ML —
"nbeats" Deep Learning torch
"nhits" Deep Learning torch
"tcn" Deep Learning torch
"tft" Deep Learning torch
"patch_tst" Deep Learning torch
"deepar" Deep Learning torch

24.4 Anomaly Detectors

Name Method
"iqr" IQR box-plot rule
"stl" STL decomposition residuals
"grubbs" Grubbs extreme-value test
"gesd" Generalised ESD test
"lof" Local Outlier Factor
"isolation_forest" Isolation Forest
"autoencoder" Deep learning autoencoder
"ensemble" Majority-vote combination

24.5 Metrics

Function Description
milt_accuracy() All metrics at once
milt_mae() Mean Absolute Error
milt_mse() Mean Squared Error
milt_rmse() Root Mean Squared Error
milt_mape() Mean Absolute % Error
milt_smape() Symmetric MAPE
milt_mase() Mean Abs Scaled Error
milt_rmsse() Root Mean Sq Scaled Error
milt_r_squared() R²
milt_crps() Continuous Ranked Probability Score
milt_coverage() Prediction interval coverage
milt_pinball() Pinball / quantile loss
milt_winkler() Winkler interval score

25 Session Info

sessionInfo()
R version 4.4.1 (2024-06-14 ucrt)
Platform: x86_64-w64-mingw32/x64
Running under: Windows 11 x64 (build 26200)

Matrix products: default

[1] LC_COLLATE=English_United Kingdom.utf8 
[2] LC_CTYPE=English_United Kingdom.utf8   
[3] LC_MONETARY=English_United Kingdom.utf8

tzcode source: internal

[1] stats     graphics  grDevices datasets  utils     methods   base     

other attached packages:
[1] milt_0.1.0     testthat_3.3.2

loaded via a namespace (and not attached):
 [1] gtable_0.3.6          anytime_0.3.12        QuickJSR_1.9.0       
 [4] xfun_0.56             ggplot2_4.0.2         htmlwidgets_1.6.4    
 [7] devtools_2.5.0        inline_0.3.21         tsibble_1.2.0        
[10] lattice_0.22-6        vctrs_0.7.2           tools_4.4.1          
[13] generics_0.1.4        stats4_4.4.1          parallel_4.4.1       
[16] tibble_3.3.1          pkgconfig_2.0.3       prophet_1.1.7        
[19] data.table_1.18.2.1   RColorBrewer_1.1-3    S7_0.2.1

[25] compiler_4.4.1        farver_2.1.2          brio_1.1.5           
[28] codetools_0.2-20      htmltools_0.5.9       usethis_3.2.1        
[31] yaml_2.3.12           pillar_1.11.1         tidyr_1.3.2          
[34] ellipsis_0.3.2        cachem_1.1.0          StanHeaders_2.32.10  
[37] sessioninfo_1.2.3     nlme_3.1-164          fracdiff_1.5-3       
[40] rstan_2.32.7          tidyselect_1.2.1      digest_0.6.39        
[43] dplyr_1.2.0           purrr_1.2.1           labeling_0.4.3       
[46] rprojroot_2.1.1       fastmap_1.2.0         grid_4.4.1

[58] lubridate_1.9.5       timechange_0.4.0      extraDistr_1.10.0.2  
[61] rmarkdown_2.30        matrixStats_1.5.0     otel_0.2.0

[70] knitr_1.51            rlang_1.1.7           Rcpp_1.1.1           
[73] glue_1.8.0            renv_1.1.8            pkgload_1.5.0
